stages.name: z3_generation

result.stdout:
(declare-const attacker_roots Int)
(declare-const total_roots Int)
(declare-const pass_threshold_num Int)
(declare-const pass_threshold_den Int)
(declare-const flash_loan_roots Int)
(declare-const honest_roots Int)
(declare-const bip_votes Int)

; Pass threshold is 50% (simplified)
(assert (= pass_threshold_num 1))
(assert (= pass_threshold_den 2))

; Honest stakers have some roots
(assert (> honest_roots 0))
(assert (= total_roots (+ honest_roots flash_loan_roots)))

; Attacker uses flash loan to acquire roots
(assert (> flash_loan_roots 0))
(assert (= attacker_roots flash_loan_roots))

; Attacker votes with flash loan roots
(assert (= bip_votes attacker_roots))

; Check if attacker can pass governance
; bip_votes / total_roots >= pass_threshold_num / pass_threshold_den
; bip_votes * pass_threshold_den >= total_roots * pass_threshold_num
(assert (>= (* bip_votes pass_threshold_den) (* total_roots pass_threshold_num)))

; This is satisfiable when flash_loan_roots >= honest_roots
; Prove: attacker needs flash_loan_roots >= honest_roots to pass
(assert (< flash_loan_roots honest_roots))

; Check emergency threshold (2/3)
(declare-const emergency_threshold_num Int)
(declare-const emergency_threshold_den Int)
(assert (= emergency_threshold_num 2))
(assert (= emergency_threshold_den 3))

; For emergency commit: bip_votes * 3 >= total_roots * 2
(assert (>= (* bip_votes emergency_threshold_den) (* total_roots emergency_threshold_num)))

; This requires flash_loan_roots >= 2 * honest_roots
(assert (< flash_loan_roots (* 2 honest_roots)))

; Verify no snapshot protection exists
(declare-const snapshot_block Int)
(declare-const vote_block Int)
(assert (= snapshot_block 0)) ; No snapshot - always current balance
(assert (> vote_block 0))
; Without snapshot: voting power = current balance, not historical
; This allows flash loan manipulation
(check-sat)
; Expected: sat for normal threshold when flash_loan >= honest_roots
; Expected: unsat for emergency when flash_loan < 2*honest_roots

result.stdout: {"matrix": [[10, -5, 0], [-8, 2, 1], [3, -1, 0]], "players": ["Attacker", "Protocol"], "strategies": ["FlashLoanGovAttack", "HonestParticipation", "GriefProposer", "Defend_Snapshot", "Defend_Timelock", "NoDefense"], "description": "3x3 payoff matrix (rows=Attacker strategies, cols=Protocol defense strategies). Row 1 (FlashLoanGovAttack): vs NoDefense=+10 (attacker drains protocol), vs Defend_Snapshot=-5 (attack fails, attacker loses flash loan fees), vs Defend_Timelock=0 (partial mitigation, users can exit). Row 2 (HonestParticipation): vs NoDefense=-8 (honest actors lose to attackers), vs Defend_Snapshot=+2 (honest governance works), vs Defend_Timelock=+1 (some protection). Row 3 (GriefProposer): vs NoDefense=+3 (lock proposer funds), vs Defend_Snapshot=-1 (snapshot prevents griefing), vs Defend_Timelock=0 (no effect). Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defenses, the dominant attacker strategy is FlashLoanGovAttack (payoff 10 > -8 > 3). The protocol's dominant strategy should be Defend_Snapshot as it minimizes attacker payoff across all strategies (-5, 2, -1) compared to NoDefense (10, -8, 3). The mixed strategy equilibrium suggests the protocol should implement BOTH snapshot AND timelock defenses to achieve the minimax outcome. The current contract has NO snapshot and NO timelock, placing it in the worst-case (NoDefense) column, making FlashLoanGovAttack the rational dominant strategy for any well-capitalized attacker. Expected value for attacker with flash loan: E[attack] = p(success)*V(protocol) - cost(flash_loan_fee), where p(success) approaches 1.0 without snapshot protection."}

game_model.description: 3x3 payoff matrix (rows=Attacker strategies, cols=Protocol defense strategies). Row 1 (FlashLoanGovAttack): vs NoDefense=+10 (attacker drains protocol), vs Defend_Snapshot=-5 (attack fails, attacker loses flash loan fees), vs Defend_Timelock=0 (partial mitigation, users can exit). Row 2 (HonestParticipation): vs NoDefense=-8 (honest actors lose to attackers), vs Defend_Snapshot=+2 (honest governance works), vs Defend_Timelock=+1 (some protection). Row 3 (GriefProposer): vs NoDefense=+3 (lock proposer funds), vs Defend_Snapshot=-1 (snapshot prevents griefing), vs Defend_Timelock=0 (no effect). Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defenses, the dominant attacker strategy is FlashLoanGovAttack (payoff 10 > -8 > 3). The protocol's dominant strategy should be Defend_Snapshot as it minimizes attacker payoff across all strategies (-5, 2, -1) compared to NoDefense (10, -8, 3). The mixed strategy equilibrium suggests the protocol should implement BOTH snapshot AND timelock defenses to achieve the minimax outcome. The current contract has NO snapshot and NO timelock, placing it in the worst-case (NoDefense) column, making FlashLoanGovAttack the rational dominant strategy for any well-capitalized attacker. Expected value for attacker with flash loan: E[attack] = p(success)*V(protocol) - cost(flash_loan_fee), where p(success) approaches 1.0 without snapshot protection.

differential.message: No voting power constraints to verify

counterexample.reason: one-time defection payoff exceeds cooperation value

evolutionary_stability.message: All honest strategies are evolutionarily stable

patch.root_cause: The governance contract has two critical incentive compatibility violations: (1) Flash loan governance attacks are possible because voting power (Stalk/Roots) is measured at the time of voting without requiring tokens to be locked for the duration of the proposal, allowing an attacker to borrow tokens, vote, and return them within the same transaction. (2) Grief proposer attacks are possible because the proposer's vote cannot be unvoted, but there is no mechanism preventing a malicious actor from griefing proposals at low cost. Additionally, the repeated game analysis shows defection dominates cooperation, indicating the lock mechanism (votedUntil/proposedUntil) is insufficient to deter attacks.
patch.vulnerability: 1. FlashLoanGovAttack: An attacker can use a flash loan to temporarily acquire large amounts of Stalk/Roots, vote on a malicious proposal, and repay the loan within the same transaction. The contract checks balanceOfRoots at vote time but does not snapshot or lock voting power against flash loan manipulation. 2. GriefProposer: An attacker with minimal stake can propose malicious BIPs repeatedly (up to the notTooProposed limit) to grief legitimate governance, and the honest participation payoff is negative (-2.2) while griefing yields positive payoff (0.6). The proposedUntil lock is insufficient deterrence. The voteAll function also fails to add roots to the BIP's vote tally (only calls recordVote but not the stalk-weighted vote accumulation), and the voteUnvoteAll function has the same issue with updateVotedUntil not properly accounting for new votes.
patch.patch_description: 1. Add a snapshot mechanism that records voting power at proposal creation time and uses that snapshot for vote weight calculation, preventing flash loan attacks by requiring that the voter's roots were present before the proposal was created. 2. Increase the proposal threshold and add a proposal deposit/bond that is slashed if the proposal fails to reach quorum, making griefing economically irrational. 3. Fix the voteAll function to properly accumulate weighted votes. 4. Add a minimum lock period check to ensure voting power cannot be acquired and used within the same block as voting. 5. Use a governance token snapshot (block number based) to prevent flash loan manipulation.

stages.name: z3_generation

result.stdout:
(declare-const roots_attacker Int)
(declare-const roots_total Int)
(declare-const threshold_emergency Decimal)
(declare-const threshold_pass Decimal)
(declare-const flash_loan_amount Int)
(declare-const honest_roots Int)

; Flash loan attack model
; Attacker borrows flash_loan_amount worth of Stalk
(assert (> flash_loan_amount 0))
(assert (> honest_roots 0))

; Attacker's effective roots after flash loan
(assert (= roots_attacker (+ honest_roots flash_loan_amount)))

; Total roots in system
(assert (= roots_total (+ roots_attacker (- honest_roots honest_roots))))

; Emergency threshold is 2/3 (0.666...)
; Attack succeeds if attacker_roots / total_roots >= 2/3
; i.e., 3 * roots_attacker >= 2 * roots_total
(declare-const attack_succeeds Bool)
(assert (= attack_succeeds (>= (* 3 roots_attacker) (* 2 roots_total))))

; Prove: if flash_loan_amount >= 2 * honest_roots, attack succeeds
(assert (>= flash_loan_amount (* 2 honest_roots)))
(assert (not attack_succeeds))

; This should be UNSAT - proving attack always succeeds with sufficient flash loan
(check-sat)

; Incentive overflow model
(declare-const base_incentive Int)
(declare-const incentive_time Int)
(declare-const max_safe_incentive Int)
(assert (= base_incentive 100))
(assert (> incentive_time 0))
(assert (= max_safe_incentive (^ 2 256)))
; fracExp(100, 100, t, 2) grows as 100 * (1.01)^t
; For t > 1000, this exceeds reasonable bounds
(assert (> incentive_time 1000))
(declare-const computed_incentive Int)
; Simplified: computed_incentive = base * (101/100)^incentive_time
; Check if it can exceed max_safe_incentive
(assert (< computed_incentive max_safe_incentive))
(check-sat)

(declare-const attack_succeeds Bool)

result.stdout: {"matrix": [[100, -50, 0], [-100, 10, 5], [0, -10, 2]], "players": ["Attacker", "Protocol"], "strategies": ["FlashLoanExploit", "HonestParticipation", "Defend_Snapshot"], "description": "3x3 payoff matrix where rows=Attacker strategies [FlashLoanExploit, HonestParticipation, WaitForIncentive], columns=Protocol strategies [NoDefense, SnapshotVoting, TimelockDelay]. Payoffs represent net value in protocol units. (1) FlashLoanExploit vs NoDefense: Attacker gains 100 (full protocol control), Protocol loses 100. (2) FlashLoanExploit vs SnapshotVoting: Attacker loses 50 (flash loan costs, failed attack), Protocol gains 10 (fees). (3) FlashLoanExploit vs TimelockDelay: Attacker gains 0 (blocked), Protocol gains 5 (deterrence). (4) HonestParticipation vs NoDefense: Attacker gains -100 (opportunity cost), Protocol gains 10. (5) WaitForIncentive vs NoDefense: Attacker gains 0 (small incentive), Protocol loses 10 (inflation). Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defense (NoDefense column), Attacker's dominant strategy is FlashLoanExploit (100 > -100 > 0). Protocol's dominant strategy is SnapshotVoting as it minimizes maximum loss. The unique Nash Equilibrium is (FlashLoanExploit, SnapshotVoting) with payoff (-50, 10), but this is only stable if snapshot mechanism is implemented. Current contract has NO snapshot mechanism, meaning the game is stuck at (FlashLoanExploit, NoDefense) = (100, -100), a catastrophic outcome for the protocol. Recommended fix: implement Compound-style checkpointing to shift equilibrium to the safer (HonestParticipation, SnapshotVoting) = (-100, 10) outcome."}

game_model.description: 3x3 payoff matrix where rows=Attacker strategies [FlashLoanExploit, HonestParticipation, WaitForIncentive], columns=Protocol strategies [NoDefense, SnapshotVoting, TimelockDelay]. Payoffs represent net value in protocol units. (1) FlashLoanExploit vs NoDefense: Attacker gains 100 (full protocol control), Protocol loses 100. (2) FlashLoanExploit vs SnapshotVoting: Attacker loses 50 (flash loan costs, failed attack), Protocol gains 10 (fees). (3) FlashLoanExploit vs TimelockDelay: Attacker gains 0 (blocked), Protocol gains 5 (deterrence). (4) HonestParticipation vs NoDefense: Attacker gains -100 (opportunity cost), Protocol gains 10. (5) WaitForIncentive vs NoDefense: Attacker gains 0 (small incentive), Protocol loses 10 (inflation). Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defense (NoDefense column), Attacker's dominant strategy is FlashLoanExploit (100 > -100 > 0). Protocol's dominant strategy is SnapshotVoting as it minimizes maximum loss. The unique Nash Equilibrium is (FlashLoanExploit, SnapshotVoting) with payoff (-50, 10), but this is only stable if snapshot mechanism is implemented. Current contract has NO snapshot mechanism, meaning the game is stuck at (FlashLoanExploit, NoDefense) = (100, -100), a catastrophic outcome for the protocol. Recommended fix: implement Compound-style checkpointing to shift equilibrium to the safer (HonestParticipation, SnapshotVoting) = (-100, 10) outcome.

counterexample.reason: one-time defection payoff exceeds cooperation value

evolutionary_stability.message: All honest strategies are evolutionarily stable

patch.root_cause: The governance contract has two critical vulnerabilities: (1) Flash loan attack vector - voting power (Stalk/Roots) is measured at the time of voting without requiring a time-lock or snapshot mechanism, allowing an attacker to acquire large amounts of Stalk via flash loans, vote on a malicious BIP, then return the funds in the same transaction. (2) Incentive incompatibility in repeated games - the payoff structure makes defection (attacking) dominant over honest participation because the cost of honest participation (-29.0) is higher than the cost of attacking (-10.0 for flash loan exploit, -0.6 for snapshot defense), meaning rational actors prefer to attack rather than participate honestly.
patch.vulnerability: 1. Flash loan governance attack: The contract uses current balance of roots (balanceOfRoots) to determine voting power without any snapshot or time-weighted mechanism. An attacker can borrow tokens, deposit to get Stalk/Roots, vote on a malicious proposal, then withdraw and repay the flash loan - all in one transaction. 2. Missing voting lock enforcement in voteAll: The voteAll function updates votedUntil but doesn't call _vote which properly handles the stalk locking mechanism. 3. The emergencyCommit and pauseOrUnpause functions allow execution while a BIP is still active, creating a race condition. 4. No minimum staking period before voting is allowed, enabling flash loan attacks.
patch.patch_description: 1. Add a snapshot mechanism that records voting power at BIP creation time rather than at vote time. 2. Add a minimum deposit age requirement before tokens can be used for voting (anti-flash-loan). 3. Fix the voteAll function to properly use _vote internal logic including stalk locking. 4. Add a depositedAt timestamp tracking to prevent same-block voting after deposits. 5. Implement proper incentive structure where honest participation is rewarded more than attacking strategies.

stages.name: z3_generation

result.stdout:
(declare-const attacker_roots Int)
(declare-const total_roots Int)
(declare-const threshold_percent Int)
(declare-const bip_votes Int)
(declare-const flash_loan_amount Int)
(declare-const normal_attacker_roots Int)
(declare-const emergency_threshold Int)

; Emergency threshold is 2/3 (66%)
(assert (= emergency_threshold 66))

; Normal attacker roots without flash loan
(assert (= normal_attacker_roots 100))

; Total roots in protocol
(assert (= total_roots 10000))

; Flash loan gives attacker massive temporary roots
(assert (= flash_loan_amount 20000))

; Attacker roots with flash loan
(assert (= attacker_roots (+ normal_attacker_roots flash_loan_amount)))

; BIP votes after flash loan attack
(assert (= bip_votes attacker_roots))

; Check if attacker can reach emergency threshold
; threshold_percent = bip_votes * 100 / (total_roots + flash_loan_amount)
(assert (= threshold_percent (div (* bip_votes 100) (+ total_roots flash_loan_amount))))

; Can attacker reach 66% threshold?
(assert (>= threshold_percent emergency_threshold))

; Verify attack is profitable: attacker needs bip_votes/total_effective >= 0.66
; 20100 / 30000 = 67% > 66% threshold
(check-sat)
; Expected: sat - attack is feasible

; Additional constraint: without flash loan
(push)
(assert (= bip_votes normal_attacker_roots))
(assert (= threshold_percent (div (* bip_votes 100) total_roots)))
(assert (>= threshold_percent emergency_threshold))
(check-sat)
; Expected: unsat - attack not feasible without flash loan
(pop)

result.stdout: {"matrix": [[100, -50, 0], [-100, 10, 5]], "players": ["Attacker", "Protocol"], "strategies": ["FlashLoanAttack", "HonestParticipation", "Defend(Snapshot+Timelock)"], "description": "Game theory analysis of the governance attack scenario. Players: Attacker (seeks to extract value) and Protocol (seeks to maintain integrity). Attacker strategies: (1) FlashLoanAttack - borrow large Stalk, vote on malicious BIP, execute emergencyCommit, drain protocol; (2) HonestParticipation - participate normally in governance. Protocol strategies: (1) No defense (current state) - vulnerable to flash loan attack; (2) Implement snapshot voting + mandatory timelock between vote and execution. Payoff matrix (Attacker, Protocol): [FlashLoanAttack vs NoDefense] = (100, -100): Attacker extracts full protocol value, protocol loses everything. [FlashLoanAttack vs Defend] = (-50, 10): Attacker loses flash loan fees and gas, protocol gains security. [HonestParticipation vs NoDefense] = (0, 5): Normal governance, small protocol gain from participation. [HonestParticipation vs Defend] = (5, 10): Both benefit from legitimate governance. Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defense, the dominant strategy for a rational attacker is FlashLoanAttack (100 > 0). The protocol's dominant strategy should be Defend (-100 < 10 when attacker attacks). The unique Nash Equilibrium is (FlashLoanAttack, NoDefense) = (100, -100) in the current implementation, which is catastrophic. Implementing snapshot voting shifts the equilibrium to (HonestParticipation, Defend) = (5, 10), a Pareto improvement. The protocol must implement: (1) ERC20Snapshot or Compound-style checkpoints for vote weight, (2) Minimum lock period between vote recording and BIP execution, (3) Quorum requirements based on historical average participation, (4) Rate limiting on large Stalk acquisitions before voting."}

game_model.description: Game theory analysis of the governance attack scenario. Players: Attacker (seeks to extract value) and Protocol (seeks to maintain integrity). Attacker strategies: (1) FlashLoanAttack - borrow large Stalk, vote on malicious BIP, execute emergencyCommit, drain protocol; (2) HonestParticipation - participate normally in governance. Protocol strategies: (1) No defense (current state) - vulnerable to flash loan attack; (2) Implement snapshot voting + mandatory timelock between vote and execution. Payoff matrix (Attacker, Protocol): [FlashLoanAttack vs NoDefense] = (100, -100): Attacker extracts full protocol value, protocol loses everything. [FlashLoanAttack vs Defend] = (-50, 10): Attacker loses flash loan fees and gas, protocol gains security. [HonestParticipation vs NoDefense] = (0, 5): Normal governance, small protocol gain from participation. [HonestParticipation vs Defend] = (5, 10): Both benefit from legitimate governance. Nash Equilibrium analysis: Without defense, the dominant strategy for a rational attacker is FlashLoanAttack (100 > 0). The protocol's dominant strategy should be Defend (-100 < 10 when attacker attacks). The unique Nash Equilibrium is (FlashLoanAttack, NoDefense) = (100, -100) in the current implementation, which is catastrophic. Implementing snapshot voting shifts the equilibrium to (HonestParticipation, Defend) = (5, 10), a Pareto improvement. The protocol must implement: (1) ERC20Snapshot or Compound-style checkpoints for vote weight, (2) Minimum lock period between vote recording and BIP execution, (3) Quorum requirements based on historical average participation, (4) Rate limiting on large Stalk acquisitions before voting.
